title: Dissociative recombination and ion-pair formation in $\mathrm{HeH^+}$ isotopologues: A time-dependent wave-packet study including rotational coupling
authors: Sifiso Musa Nkambule, Malibongwe Tsabedze, Oscar N. Mabuza, Mbuso K. Matfunjwa
abstract: We present a comprehensive theoretical investigation of dissociative recombination (DR) and resonant ion-pair (RIP) formation in $\mathrm{HeH^+}$ isotopologues using time-dependent wave-packet propagation methods. Nuclear dynamics are treated on a set of 23 coupled electronic states, including $^2Σ$, $^2Π$, and $^2Δ$ symmetries, in both adiabatic and strictly diabatic representations, with rotational couplings explicitly included. Reaction cross sections are computed over collision energies ranging from 0 to 50 eV.   The results reveal that inclusion of a large manifold of resonant states and rotational couplings significantly enhances the DR cross section relative to earlier theoretical studies. In the diabatic representation, $^2Σ$ states dominate the recombination dynamics, while in the adiabatic representation, $^2Π$ and $^2Δ$ states contribute significantly at low collision energies. For RIP formation, two different diabatization schemes yield systematically larger cross sections than previous models, highlighting the sensitivity of ion-pair production to electronic coupling structure.   Isotopic effects are examined, showing a clear inverse dependence of cross section magnitude on reduced mass. The present results underscore the importance of multi-state coupling and nonadiabatic effects in accurately describing electron-molecule collision processes in primordial and astrophysical plasmas.
summary: This theoretical study uses time-dependent wave-packet methods to calculate the rates for dissociative recombination and ion-pair formation in $\mathrm{HeH^+}$ isotopologues. By incorporating a large number of coupled electronic states and explicit rotational couplings, the authors find that these processes are significantly more efficient than previously modeled. The results highlight the critical role of nonadiabatic coupling and mass effects in determining astrophysical reaction rates.
main_problem: The study investigates dissociative recombination (DR) and resonant ion-pair (RIP) formation processes in $\mathrm{HeH^+}$ isotopologues.
main_result: The inclusion of a large manifold of resonant states and explicit rotational couplings significantly enhances the predicted DR cross sections compared to previous theoretical models. Lighter isotopes show systematically larger reaction rates due to mass effects.
method: Time-dependent wave-packet propagation methods are employed, treating nuclear dynamics across 23 coupled electronic states in both adiabatic and diabatic representations.
model_or_system: The system is the helium hydride ion ($\mathrm{HeH^+}$) and its isotopologues. The Hamiltonian governs interactions between multiple electronic potential energy curves ($^2\Sigma, ^2\Pi, ^2\Delta$) coupled by non-adiabatic and rotational terms.
key_observables: Reaction cross sections for DR ($\sigma_{DR}$) and RIP formation ($\sigma_{RIP}$); thermal rate coefficients $k(T)$; isotopic dependence of these rates.
important_parameters: Collision energies (0 to 50 eV); electron temperature ($T$); reduced mass ($\mu$).
main_assumptions: The calculations utilize the local complex potential approximation for autoionization, which may neglect interference effects between overlapping resonances.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate total DR cross sections vs. collision energy comparing adiabatic/diabatic results; symmetry contributions to the cross section; and thermal rate coefficients across different isotopes and rotational states.
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the theoretical framework by defining the Hamiltonian, implementing wave-packet propagation in both representations, calculating observables (cross sections), analyzing physical effects (symmetry, mass dependence), and finally deriving astrophysical rates.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics as it models complex electron-molecule collision dynamics involving multiple coupled electronic states, nonadiabatic transitions, and rotational coupling—hallmarks of molecular quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 5 shows the resonant ion-pair (RIP) formation cross sections obtained using Method 1, while Figure 6 compares the two diabatization procedures. A major result of the present work is that both dia- batization approaches predict RIP cross sections signif- icantly larger than those reported previously by Larson et al. [13]. The enhancement is particularly pronounced at low collision energies where previous calculations pre- dicted almost negligible ion-pair formation.
